edges.description: Prothrombotic states can produce hepatic vein thrombosis, the classic obstructive lesion in primary Budd-Chiari syndrome.
edges.source: Systemic Prothrombotic Predisposition

edges.description: Prothrombotic states promote hepatic venous thrombosis or fibrous sequelae that block hepatic venous outflow.
edges.source: Systemic Prothrombotic Predisposition

edges.description: Hepatic venous obstruction increases sinusoidal pressure, impairs portal flow, and causes congestive liver injury.
edges.source: Hepatic Venous Outflow Obstruction
edges.target: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Hepatic venous congestion increases intrahepatic resistance and portal pressure.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Raised hepatic sinusoidal pressure drives fluid filtration into the peritoneal cavity.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Passive hepatic congestion enlarges the liver.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Portal hypertension produces congestive splenic enlargement.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Congestive hepatocyte injury can impair bilirubin handling and produce jaundice.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Portal hypertension diverts blood through gastroesophageal collateral veins.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Variceal rupture or portal-hypertensive bleeding can manifest as gastrointestinal hemorrhage.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Portal hypertension and hepatic dysfunction can produce dependent edema.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Hypoxic congestive liver injury elevates circulating hepatocellular injury enzymes.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Congestive hepatobiliary injury can elevate circulating alkaline phosphatase.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Severe liver dysfunction from venous congestion can progress to hepatic encephalopathy.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Persistent venous congestion and hepatocyte hypoxic injury can progress to fibrosis, cirrhosis, and liver failure.
edges.source: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.description: Persistent congestive fibrosis can progress to cirrhosis.
edges.source: Congestive Hepatic Fibrosis

edges.description: Fulminant congestive hepatic injury can progress to acute hepatic failure.
edges.source: Congestive Hepatic Fibrosis
Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt
edges.target: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

edges.source: Angioplasty for Short Venous Stenosis
Liver Transplantation

nodes.description: Acute hepatic failure is an occasional severe presentation or complication of Budd-Chiari syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0006554 | Acute hepatic failure | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Angioplasty is used in the stepwise strategy when short hepatic venous or inferior vena cava stenoses are anatomically suitable for recanalization.

evidence.snippet: A therapeutic strategy has been proposed where anticoagulation, correction of risk factors, diuretics and prophylaxis for portal hypertension are used first; then angioplasty for short-length venous stenoses; then TIPS; and ultimately liver transplantation.

nodes.description: Ascites is the hallmark feature of Budd-Chiari syndrome, arising when hepatic venous outflow obstruction raises sinusoidal pressure and drives filtration of protein-rich interstitial fluid into the peritoneal cavity. The resulting progressive abdominal distension is often the presenting complaint and may be refractory until the venous obstruction is relieved.

evidence.snippet: The typical and most common form of clinical presentation of BCS is the chronic one, with a slow-onset pain in the right upper abdomen, jaundice (not always present in chronic form), hepatosplenomegaly, progressive abdominal swelling/stretching (due to ascites), haematemesis (due to esophageal varices caused by portal hypertension);
HP:0001541 | Ascites | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Bipedal edema is a frequent dependent-edema manifestation in Budd-Chiari syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0033045 | Bipedal edema | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Chronic hepatic venous congestion and fibrosis can progress to cirrhosis.

evidence.snippet: HP:0001394 | Cirrhosis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Untreated hepatic venous outflow obstruction can cause rapid congestive fibrosis and progressive liver failure.

evidence.snippet: If left untreated, the natural course of the disease is extremely unfavorable with a mortality rate of 50% in 2 years, while the 3-year survival rate of untreated patients is < 10%, as the venous outflow obstruction leads to hepatic congestion and fulminant fibrosis, typically within 3 mo[11].

nodes.description: Elevated circulating alkaline phosphatase is a frequent laboratory manifestation of Budd-Chiari syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0003155 | Elevated circulating alkaline phosphatase concentration | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Elevated circulating hepatic transaminases reflect hepatocyte injury in Budd-Chiari syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002910 | Elevated circulating hepatic transaminase concentration | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Esophageal varices form as portosystemic collaterals when the portal hypertension of Budd-Chiari syndrome diverts blood through the gastroesophageal venous plexus. These dilated submucosal veins are prone to rupture, and variceal bleeding presenting as hematemesis is a major cause of morbidity in the chronic disease.

evidence.snippet: The typical and most common form of clinical presentation of BCS is the chronic one, with a slow-onset pain in the right upper abdomen, jaundice (not always present in chronic form), hepatosplenomegaly, progressive abdominal swelling/stretching (due to ascites), haematemesis (due to esophageal varices caused by portal hypertension);

nodes.description: Gastrointestinal hemorrhage can occur as a complication of portal hypertension and variceal bleeding.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002239 | Gastrointestinal hemorrhage | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Obstruction of hepatic venous drainage raises sinusoidal pressure and causes hepatic congestion.

evidence.snippet: Primary Budd-Chiari syndrome is characterized by a blocked hepatic venous outflow tract at various levels from small hepatic veins to inferior vena cava, resulting from thrombosis or its fibrous sequellae.
BCS develops from a spectrum of diseases determining hepatic venous outflow obstruction, both thrombotic and non-thrombotic.

nodes.description: Hepatic encephalopathy can occur with severe hepatic dysfunction in Budd-Chiari syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0002480 | Hepatic encephalopathy | Occasional (29-5%)

nodes.description: Hepatic vein thrombosis is the classic thrombotic lesion producing primary Budd-Chiari syndrome.

evidence.snippet: HP:0030243 | Hepatic vein thrombosis | Frequent (79-30%)

nodes.description: Hepatomegaly in Budd-Chiari syndrome results from passive congestion of the liver behind the obstructed hepatic venous outflow tract, with engorged sinusoids and interstitial edema enlarging the organ. The caudate lobe, which often drains directly into the inferior vena cava, may hypertrophy and become especially prominent.

evidence.snippet: The typical and most common form of clinical presentation of BCS is the chronic one, with a slow-onset pain in the right upper abdomen, jaundice (not always present in chronic form), hepatosplenomegaly, progressive abdominal swelling/stretching (due to ascites), haematemesis (due to esophageal varices caused by portal hypertension);

nodes.description: Jaundice in Budd-Chiari syndrome reflects hepatocellular dysfunction from congestive, hypoxic liver injury behind the obstructed venous outflow. It is variable and not always present in the chronic form, becoming more prominent in acute or fulminant presentations with significant hepatic impairment.

evidence.snippet: The typical and most common form of clinical presentation of BCS is the chronic one, with a slow-onset pain in the right upper abdomen, jaundice (not always present in chronic form), hepatosplenomegaly, progressive abdominal swelling/stretching (due to ascites), haematemesis (due to esophageal varices caused by portal hypertension);

nodes.description: Liver transplantation is reserved for refractory or advanced Budd-Chiari syndrome when medical and endovascular strategies fail or liver failure is advanced.

evidence.snippet: A therapeutic strategy has been proposed where anticoagulation, correction of risk factors, diuretics and prophylaxis for portal hypertension are used first; then angioplasty for shortlength venous stenosis; then Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt (TIPS); and ultimately liver transplantation.

nodes.description: Portal hypertension in Budd-Chiari syndrome arises directly from the increased intrahepatic resistance produced by hepatic venous outflow obstruction and sinusoidal congestion. Sustained elevation of portal pressure underlies the ascites, splenomegaly, and varices that characterize the disorder and is a primary target for prophylaxis and shunting therapy.

evidence.snippet: A therapeutic strategy has been proposed where anticoagulation, correction of risk factors, diuretics and prophylaxis for portal hypertension are used first; then angioplasty for shortlength venous stenosis; then Transjugular Intrahepatic Portosystemic Shunt (TIPS); and ultimately liver transplantation.
HP:0001409 | Portal hypertension | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Sustained hepatic venous congestion drives portal hypertension, ascites, hepatomegaly, and progressive liver dysfunction.
nodes.id: Portal Hypertension and Congestive Liver Injury

evidence.snippet: The obstruction of the hepatic veins results in increase of hepatic sinusoidal pressure, sinusoid dilation, and filtration of interstitial fluid, which leads to ascites; in addition, there is increase in the intrahepatic resistances and, therefore, decrease in portal venous flow, leading to hypoxic damage of hepatocytes[14].

nodes.description: Splenomegaly develops in Budd-Chiari syndrome as a consequence of the portal hypertension that follows hepatic venous outflow obstruction, with congestion and passive engorgement of the spleen. It is frequently noted together with hepatomegaly as hepatosplenomegaly in the chronic presentation.

evidence.snippet: The typical and most common form of clinical presentation of BCS is the chronic one, with a slow-onset pain in the right upper abdomen, jaundice (not always present in chronic form), hepatosplenomegaly, progressive abdominal swelling/stretching (due to ascites), haematemesis (due to esophageal varices caused by portal hypertension);
HP:0001744 | Splenomegaly | Very frequent (99-80%)

nodes.description: Primary Budd-Chiari syndrome commonly occurs in patients with acquired or inherited prothrombotic conditions, especially myeloproliferative disorders.

evidence.snippet: Multiple risk factors have been identified and are often combined in the same patient.

Myeloproliferative diseases of atypical presentation account for nearly 50% of patients; their diagnosis can be made by showing the V617F mutation in Janus tyrosine kinase-2 gene of peripheral blood granulocytes and, should this mutation be absent, by showing clusters of dystrophic megacaryocytes at bone marrow biopsy.

nodes.description: TIPS can decompress portal hypertension and restore effective hepatic outflow in selected patients.

evidence.snippet: TIPS is today considered a safe and highly effective treatment and should be recommended for BCS patients, including those awaiting orthotopic liver transplantation.
